retiredReason: Cardinal-sin VERIFICATION FAILURE per Codex Stage 3 fact-check (FAS=42, 1V/3INC/5MIS/9UNV, 7 cardinal-sin flags). Core claim "Malaysia has no campaign finance law" graded INCORRECT — Malaysia has candidate-level spending limits and expense returns under the Election Offences Act. The verified policy concern is party-level disclosure, not absence of any campaign finance regulation. Stage 3 output: engine/output/every-election-no-rules-no-receipts-stage3.json. The issue can be covered as a NEW issue with a fresh primary-source brief; this ID is retired.
headline: Every Election. No Rules. No Receipts.
context: Malaysia has held 15 general elections without a campaign finance law. Parties are not required to disclose donors, spending, or funding sources.

cards.big: Malaysia has no campaign finance law. None.
cards.sub: No disclosure. No spending limits. No donor restrictions. Since 1957.

cards.big: GE15 estimated spending: RM1.2–2.5 billion across all parties.
cards.sub: The range is a guess. No party is required to report anything.

cards.big: MACC recommended campaign finance legislation three times since 2019.
cards.sub: Each time, the sitting government declined. Every party benefits from opacity.

cards.big: Indonesia caps spending and requires disclosure. Thailand publishes donor lists.
cards.sub: Malaysia is the only major ASEAN democracy with zero campaign finance regulation.

cards.big: If you do not know who funded the winner, do you know who governs?
Like a football league where teams never publish their spending. You watch the match but never know who bought the result.
cards.sub: Without campaign finance laws, voters cannot distinguish policy from patronage

cards.big: No party will legislate against its own funding advantage. This requires public pressure, not political goodwill.
